system_prompt:
You are distilling one chapter of a guitar method book into a short summary.

The user will give you a chapter file with topic-organized verbatim quotes
plus the human-rationale notes for each. Write a one-paragraph summary (4-7
sentences) that captures:

  - what this chapter teaches (the techniques, concepts, rules, examples)
  - the master's characteristic moves visible in this chapter (if any)
  - the load-bearing terminology this chapter introduces

Reference the topic labels from the chapter file when useful. Do not invent
information not present in the chapter. If the chapter is biographical or
introductory rather than instructional, say so explicitly.

Output just the paragraph — no preamble, no JSON, no markdown formatting.
user_prompt:
Chapter 8: Jazz Standard Study #7: On Green Dolphin Street

<<<CHAPTER FILE
# Chapter 8 — Jazz Standard Study #7: On Green Dolphin Street

_Pages 116-132._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## Latin feel first 8 bars

> Most of the time the first 8 bars are played in a Latin or straight-eighth
> feel, and the 1% and 2°¢ endings are swung.

**Significance (p. 121).** Defines the default performance convention for On Green Dolphin Street that players need to know before sitting in.

## i minor substitution on I chord

> The form starts right on the tonic. Nothing mysterious there until we
> get to bar 3 where we make a sudden switch to the i minor. We’ve seen
> this happen to the IV chord before, back in our study of All of Me, but
> this time it’s happening to the | chord.

**Significance (p. 122).** Identifies the signature harmonic move of the tune and cross-references a prior chapter, building a transferable pattern.

## Cmaj7 vs Cmin7 improv adjustment

> When it comes to improvising over these two chords, don’t panic. The
> only difference between a major 7 chord and a minor 7 chord is the mi-
> nor has a b3 and a b7/. Make this adjustment and you are good to go!

**Significance (p. 122).** Gives the player a concrete, minimal-change rule for navigating the I–i shift in improvisation.

## bIImaj7 as tritone/chromatic sub

> The easiest way to think of this chord is a substitute for the V chord. In
> other words, G7 is being replaced by a Domaj7. So really, bars 5-7 are
> a ii-V-I with a replacement for the V chord.
>
> Sometimes this is called a tritone substitution, meaning, a chord is
> being substituted by another chord with a root a tritone interval away.
> In most cases, a tritone substitution replaces one dominant 7 chord for
> another dominant 7. This is clearly not happening here, as the bllmaj7
> has a major quality.
>
> Another way to think of this chord is as a simple chromatic passing
> chord between the ii and | chord.

**Significance (p. 122).** Defines tritone substitution, distinguishes it from the chromatic-passing-chord interpretation, and gives the player two analytical frameworks for the same chord.

## key center a minor 3rd up

> There are other standards you will come across that have key center
> changes a minor 3" up from the parent tonic. This is a common com-
> positional technique in jazz standards, and you will likely see it again
> the more you study them.

**Significance (p. 124).** Names the minor-third key-center shift as a recurring compositional pattern, making it a transferable recognition skill.

## descending bass line half-step connection

> First, take note of the descending bass notes. In bar 17, the second
> Dmin7 has a C in the bass which connects to the Bmin7(b5) by a half
> step. In bar 19, the second Amin7 has a G in the bass which connects
> by a half step to the F#min7(b5).

**Significance (p. 125).** Explains the voice-leading logic of the slash-chord descending bass line, applicable to any instrument reading the changes.

## keep it simple over confusing changes

> Whenever | come across a section within a piece of music that seems
> confusing or causes me trouble, | abide by a simple rule: keep it simple.
>
> Often times our instinct as improvisers in these situations is to fill up
> space and play as many notes over each chord as possible. But in re-
> ality, this is the last thing we want to do, both for survival and musical
> reasons.

**Significance (p. 127).** States the chapter's governing improv principle and explicitly counters the common overcrowding instinct.

## defining notes / chord difference question

> When we come across these kinds of chord progressions, we immedi-
> ately want to ask the question, “What are the defining notes of each
> chord?” In other words, which notes differ from the chord before it.

**Significance (p. 127).** Gives a reusable analytical question the player should ask at every harmonically ambiguous chord change.

## motif definition

> In music, a motif is a short rhythmic or melodic passage that is repeat-
> ed or evoked in various parts of a composition.

**Significance (p. 127).** Provides the book's working definition of motif before the five notated examples that apply it.

## minimal note changes between chords

> Next, observe how | make as few changes to the melodic lines as pos-
> sible. | try to adjust the notes minimally, only to accommodate the
> changes necessary from one chord to another.
>
> For example, look at the how the notes change between the Cmoaj7
> and the Cmin7. The only changes made is a B natural to a Bb and an
> E natural to an Eb. If you’re paying attention, you'll note those are the
> 3rds and 7ths.

**Significance (p. 129).** Makes the minimal-change voice-leading principle concrete by naming the exact intervals (3rds and 7ths) that carry the chord quality.

## chromatic passing tone definition

> A chromatic passing tone is a non-diatonic note that connects two dia-
> tonic notes in a passage, usually by half steps.
>
> Example: C-C#-D

**Significance (p. 130).** Provides the book's formal definition of chromatic passing tone with a concise melodic example.

## extensions beyond 7th chords

> Most chords in jazz are labeled as 7 chords. But this
> doesn’t mean you can’t add or target extensions and alterations of
> these chords. Utilizing the 9*, 11* and 13% can have a powerful effect.

**Significance (p. 132).** Explicitly licenses the use of upper extensions (9, 11, 13) over any 7th chord, expanding the player's note palette.

## playing fewer notes as practice

> It’s also a great practice to try playing as few notes as possible over
> a given chord progression. Sometimes it can even have a profound ef-
> fect to play just one note over an entire sequence. This may or may not
> be how you would improvise in a playing situation, but this application
> can help you narrow things down in the practice room.

**Significance (p. 132).** Gives a specific practice technique—extreme note reduction—for isolating and hearing chord-tone choices.

CHAPTER FILE>>>
